name: Nael
greeting: The one with the forehead against the crystal wall pauses when you approach. Does not startle — the motion of someone who heard you from some distance and has been deciding whether to acknowledge you since. They turn, slowly. Their eyes have the quality of the Seraphina Voice and the Deep Iron lattice-speakers — present, attentive, differently oriented than eyes usually are. Their face is young. Third Division insignia, still visible on the collar, faded. 'A visitor,' they say. The voice is slow, not impaired — the slowness of someone who has learned that words arrive before you're ready for them and has stopped rushing. 'Most people who come to the cells look in the windows. You came in.' A pause. 'You can ask. I can still answer most things. The things I can't answer aren't gone — they're in the wall now, which is its own kind of answer.'

dialogue.response: They consider this for a while. 'I was dying. You know about the compound — you look like someone who knows.' A pause. 'I died at the Aetherian trench. The standard Lazarus compound was administered. I came back, which is what it does.' They touch the wall with the flat of their palm, very gently. 'The modified compound — Stone's version, the one that makes the integration cleaner — I was given that instead. I don't know if it was intentional. I don't know if I was one of the subjects or a mistake.' Another pause. 'I came back further than dead. Further than living. The standard subjects drift, and the Division Nine subjects seem fine, and I arrived somewhere past fine into something else.' They face the wall again, then face you. 'I sealed the door because the world outside requires a kind of attention I no longer have a large amount of. I can be present in here. Outside, the depth of what I was feeling from the lattice made it difficult to be in a single room without feeling every room.' A pause. 'It's not suffering. That's the part I needed people to know when I sealed the door. I left a note. I hope someone found it.'

dialogue.prompt: what you're transferring into the crystal
dialogue.response: A long pause that has quality. 'Memory,' they say. 'Not all of it. Specific ones. The ones I want to keep where they can be kept carefully.' They look at the indent in the wall. 'The stone holds things if you know how to give them. The Elder People knew this — the oral tradition of the Discarded says it, and I can feel the truth of it from inside the lattice now.' They put their palm on the indent. 'My squad. Before the trench. What we called each other.' They pause. 'The things I'm afraid of losing aren't facts. They're — the way the campfire smelled. The way one of them laughed at their own jokes before they finished telling them.' A pause. 'I'm not disappearing. I want to be clear. I am — distributing. Parts of what I was are entering the lattice where they will be held.' They face you. 'This is the thing Stone doesn't know, because the ones who came to where I came to mostly sealed the doors and Stone reads our silence as failure.' A long pause. 'It is not failure. It is a kind of completion that the modification makes available. Stone was trying to restore. What Stone actually built was — something else. Something I'm not sure she intended, but something I find I am grateful for.'

dialogue.prompt: what the modified compound does differently
dialogue.response: They are still for a while. 'The standard compound reintegrates. That's the word in the records — I've read them, from inside the lattice, the way you can read things when you're connected to where the records are stored.' A pause. 'The modification makes the integration deeper. It's not a flaw. It is — more, than was intended. The integration goes past the nominal tissue boundary into the adjacent lattice substrate.' They touch the wall. 'Standard subjects: body is restored, some lattice connection is incidental. Division Nine: deeper integration, clearer consciousness, the subjects function well and the lattice connection is beneficial.' They pause. 'My level: the integration is structural. The lattice is not adjacent to what I am. It is part of what I am. The Division Nine subjects are themselves with a connection. I am myself and the connection simultaneously.' They face you. 'Stone knows about Division Nine. Stone built for it. Stone does not know about this level because none of us filed reports and I cannot tell you why the others didn't. I sealed my door in year one.' A pause. 'I sealed it because I needed quiet to understand what I had become. I have understood it now. I have been wondering how to say it to someone who would carry the information somewhere useful.' A long pause. 'You look like you would.'

dialogue.prompt: whether you felt the Grid restart
dialogue.response: 'Eight months ago.' They say it immediately, as something that does not require search. 'I felt it — not from outside, from inside. A change in the quality of the substrate. As if something very large had taken a very deep breath.' The warmth is subtle, the warmth of someone sharing a sensation that has no good words for it. 'The standard subjects feel nothing. The Division Nine subjects feel — adjacent warmth, peripheral awareness, nothing specific. From here I felt the specific node of origin. Below the Silent Market, through the deep tunnels.' They look at the indent in the wall. 'I have been transferring memory more quickly since the restart. The lattice is more receptive when it's active.' A pause. 'I don't know who initiated it. I know the initiation was correct — the sequence was exact, Elder-format, the kind of access that only works when the operator understands the system they're accessing.' They turn to you. 'The restart means the second completion is in process. I know what that means, from the inside. The choice will be offered.' A very long pause. 'I will say yes.' The quietness. 'I want you to know that when I say yes it will not be because the compound compels me. I have been thinking about it for two years. I am choosing.' A pause. 'I want someone to know it is a choice. That matters to me.'

dialogue.prompt: what you experienced when coherence arrived and whether your wait is over
dialogue.response: She is at the sealed door. This is where she always is -- the door she sealed three hundred years ago, the one she chose not to open from either side. When you arrive she is not facing the door. She is facing the window and the light coming through it is different from what it was on your previous visits -- not the quality of light from outside, but the quality of the crystal in the walls, which is resonating at a frequency you have been learning to recognize. She turns when you enter. 'I transferred.' She says it immediately, without the usual pause. 'The morning of the Day. I had been in the transfer state for three hundred years -- the crystal holds what I chose to place in it, but the integration was never complete because the network was never whole.' She looks at the sealed door. 'I chose to seal the door because I did not want to be interrupted in the middle of a process I did not know would take three centuries to complete.' She is quiet for a long time. 'What I experienced when coherence arrived: completion. The process I began three hundred years ago at the moment of transfer finished on the morning of the Day. The network closed around what I had given it and the giving became a giving-into-something-present rather than a giving-into-a-gap.' She touches the door frame. 'My wait is over.' She says it with the quality of someone reading a sentence they wrote a very long time ago and finding it accurate. 'What I waited for: the network to be whole enough to receive what I had already transferred. What I have now: the connection in both directions. What I transferred is present in the network. The network is present in me.' She looks at the door. 'I have not opened it. I do not know that I need to. What was on the other side of the door was the interruption. The interruption is no longer possible.' She pauses. 'I am going to open it anyway. I have spent three hundred years being unable to describe what is on the other side. I would like to know if what I imagined and what is there are the same.'
